title: Best Time to Trek to Everest Base Camp
excerpt: Choosing the right season is the single most important decision before attempting the Everest Base Camp trek. We break down every month so you know exactly when to go.
content:
## Why Season Matters on the EBC Trek

The Khumbu region experiences two ideal trekking windows and two seasons to avoid entirely.

### Spring (March–May) — Peak Season
Spring is the most popular time to trek to EBC. The skies are clear in the mornings, rhododendrons are in bloom below 4,000 m, and the days are warm. The main downside is crowds — Namche Bazaar and the teahouses fill up fast. Book well in advance.

- **March**: Perfect weather, fewer crowds than April/May. Some snow at high altitude.
- **April**: The sweet spot. Long clear days, blooming flowers, and Everest climbers establishing camps — you'll see expedition tents at base camp.
- **May**: Still good, but the pre-monsoon cloud builds in the afternoon. Avoid late May.

### Autumn (October–November) — Peak Season
Autumn is the second best window. The monsoon clears the dust and the air is crystal-sharp, delivering the most vivid mountain views of the year.

- **October**: Arguably the single best month. Stable weather, clear skies, warm days.
- **November**: Colder nights, fewer trekkers. Snow possible above 4,500 m. Still excellent.

### Monsoon (June–September) — Avoid
Heavy rain, leeches, landslides, and near-zero visibility. Not recommended unless you are specifically trekking on the rain-shadow trails of Upper Mustang.

### Winter (December–February) — For Experienced Trekkers Only
Bitterly cold at altitude (-20°C at Gorak Shep), many teahouses close above Dingboche. The trails are quiet and the views can be stunning on clear days, but experience in cold-weather camping is essential.

## Our Recommendation
Fly into Lukla in **early October** or **mid-April** for the optimum balance of weather, views, and teahouse availability.
author: Passang Sherpa

title: Beginner's Guide to Trekking in Nepal
excerpt: Never trekked before? Nepal is actually one of the friendliest destinations for first-timers. Here is everything you need to know before you lace up your boots.
content:
## You Don't Need to Be a Mountaineer

Nepal has a well-developed teahouse trekking system that makes multi-day high-altitude treks accessible to anyone with a reasonable base of fitness. You don't need technical climbing skills, you don't need to carry a tent, and you don't need to cook your own food.

## Choose the Right Trail for Your Level

| Trail | Duration | Difficulty | Max Altitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ghorepani Poon Hill | 4–5 days | Easy | 3,210 m |
| Langtang Valley | 7–10 days | Moderate | 4,773 m |
| Annapurna Base Camp | 10–12 days | Moderate | 4,130 m |
| Everest Base Camp | 14 days | Challenging | 5,364 m |

## Essential Gear

You don't need to buy everything — most equipment can be rented in Kathmandu or Pokhara.

- **Layering system**: moisture-wicking base, insulating mid-layer, waterproof shell
- **Trekking boots**: broken in before the trip — blisters at altitude are miserable
- **Trekking poles**: highly recommended, especially for the knees on descents
- **Down jacket**: essential above 3,500 m
- **Sleeping bag liner**: teahouse blankets are provided but a liner adds warmth

## Permits You'll Need

Every trek in Nepal requires at least two permits:
1. **TIMS (Trekkers' Information Management System)** card
2. **National Park / Conservation Area permit** specific to your trail

Your trekking agency will arrange these for you.

## Altitude Sickness

Acute Mountain Sickness (AMS) affects many trekkers above 3,000 m. The rule is simple: **ascend slowly, descend if symptoms worsen**. Never ascend with a headache. Stay hydrated, avoid alcohol, and consider carrying Diamox if your doctor recommends it.

## First Trek Recommendation

The **Langtang Valley trek** is our top pick for first-timers wanting a real Himalayan experience. It's accessible from Kathmandu (no domestic flight needed), moderately challenging, and far less crowded than the Everest and Annapurna routes.
author: Rina Tamang

title: Top 10 Things to Do in Pokhara
excerpt: Pokhara is Nepal's adventure capital and the gateway to the Annapurna region. From paragliding to lakeside evenings, here are the ten best experiences.
content:
## Nepal's Most Relaxed City — With a Wild Side

Pokhara sits at 827 m in a wide green valley, with the Annapurna range rising directly behind the city and Phewa Lake glittering in front of it. It's simultaneously Nepal's most laid-back lakeside town and its most adrenaline-fuelled adventure hub.

### 1. Paraglide from Sarangkot
The most popular activity in Pokhara, and for good reason. The 30–45 minute tandem flight with a certified pilot takes you over the lake with Machhapuchhre (Fishtail Mountain) filling the sky in front of you.

### 2. Sunrise at Sarangkot
Wake before dawn and hike or drive to the Sarangkot viewpoint (1,592 m) for one of Nepal's most spectacular sunrise panoramas — the entire Annapurna and Dhaulagiri ranges glow pink and gold.

### 3. Phewa Lake Boating
Rent a wooden rowing boat and paddle to the Tal Barahi temple on an island in the middle of the lake. The reflections of the mountains in the calm water are postcard-perfect.

### 4. Visit the International Mountain Museum
The world's best museum dedicated to mountaineering. Exhibits cover the history of Himalayan exploration, the lives of high-altitude porters and Sherpas, and the geology of the mountains.

### 5. Devi's Falls & Gupteshwor Cave
Devi's Falls plunges into an underground gorge and disappears — follow it into the Gupteshwor Cave system directly beneath.

### 6. Zip-line over the Valley
At 1,800 m long and with a descent of 600 m vertical metres, Pokhara's High Ground Adventures zip-line is one of the longest and steepest in the world.

### 7. White-Water Rafting on the Seti River
Half-day rafting trips on the Seti or full-day trips on the Kali Gandaki offer Grade III–IV rapids within easy reach of the city.

### 8. Trek to the Peace Pagoda
A 2-hour hike through forest brings you to the Japanese-built World Peace Pagoda on a ridge above the southern lake, with panoramic views across Pokhara.

### 9. Old Bazaar Exploration
The historic old bazaar district of Pokhara preserves centuries-old Newar architecture and a living trading culture. Excellent for photography and local street food.

### 10. Begnas Lake
Away from the Lakeside tourist strip, Begnas Lake is quiet, clean, and circled by green hills. Ideal for a half-day of boating, birdwatching, and picnicking.
author: Sanjay Gurung

title: Bhutan: Planning Your Visit to the Last Shangri-La
excerpt: Bhutan has a unique high-value, low-impact tourism policy. Understanding how it works — and what it costs — is the first step to planning your dream trip.
content:
## Why Bhutan is Different

Unlike every other country on earth, Bhutan charges all foreign tourists (except Indian, Bangladeshi, and Maldivian nationals) a mandatory **Sustainable Development Fee (SDF)** of USD 100 per person per night. This fee funds free healthcare, education, and conservation across the kingdom.

In return, you get an experience that is genuinely unspoilt: there are no billboards, no fast-food chains, and the natural environment is constitutionally protected (60% of the land must remain forested).

## Visa Requirements

You cannot visit Bhutan independently. All travellers must:
1. Obtain a Bhutan visa in advance (applied for by your licensed Bhutanese tour operator)
2. Book through a licensed Bhutanese tour operator or international partner like Hello KTM Trek
3. Have all accommodation, transport, and guide pre-booked

The visa is stamped on arrival at Paro Airport.

## What's Included in the SDF

- Government taxes
- Royalty to conservation and culture funds
- Part of your guide and official services

Not included: your flights, accommodation, food, and guide fees — these are paid to your tour operator.

## Best Time to Visit

- **Spring (March–May)**: Rhododendrons in bloom, clear skies, festivals including Paro Tsechu
- **Autumn (September–November)**: Crystal-clear mountain views, comfortable temperatures, Thimphu and Punakha Tsechu festivals
- **Winter (Dec–Feb)**: Cold but uncrowded; Takin Wildlife Reserve and Black-necked crane migration at Phobjikha

## Must-See Highlights

1. **Tiger's Nest Monastery (Paro Taktsang)**: Non-negotiable. The monastery clings to a cliff at 3,120 m and is one of Asia's most sacred and dramatic sites.
2. **Punakha Dzong**: Built at the confluence of two rivers; stunning in spring when jacaranda trees bloom purple around the fortress walls.
3. **Dochula Pass**: 108 chortens on a mountain pass with (on clear days) a breathtaking view of the eastern Himalayan peaks.
4. **Haa Valley**: A remote, rarely visited valley where traditional Bhutanese life continues unchanged.

## Our Recommendation

Allow at least 7 days. Fewer than 5 days feels rushed and doesn't justify the long-haul flight and SDF cost. The ideal itinerary covers Thimphu, Punakha, and Paro, with the Tiger's Nest hike as the centrepiece.
author: Deepika Pradhan

title: Essential Gear for High-Altitude Trekking
excerpt: Above 4,000 m, the right gear is not a luxury — it is a safety issue. This is our definitive packing list built from thousands of guided treks in the Himalayas.
content:
## The Layering System

High-altitude weather changes rapidly. A sunny afternoon at Namche Bazaar can become a blizzard by evening. The solution is a three-layer system:

### Layer 1: Base Layer (Moisture Management)
- **Merino wool** or **synthetic moisture-wicking fabric** — never cotton (cotton kills: it holds moisture and chills you)
- Long-sleeve top and full-length thermal leggings for cold nights

### Layer 2: Mid Layer (Insulation)
- **Fleece jacket** (100–200 weight) for active trekking
- **Down jacket** (600-fill or higher) for camp, evenings, and high passes
- Both are needed above 4,000 m

### Layer 3: Outer Shell (Weather Protection)
- **Gore-Tex or equivalent waterproof/breathable jacket** with a hood
- Waterproof over-trousers for monsoon trekking

## Footwear

The most important investment. Recommendations:
- **Mid-cut or high-cut waterproof trekking boots** — ankle support is critical on rocky trails
- Break them in for at least 100 km before the trek
- **Trekking sandals or camp shoes**: your feet will thank you every evening
- **Wool trekking socks**: at least 3–4 pairs; merino wool resists odour

## Sleeping

Teahouses provide blankets, but above 4,000 m:
- A **sleeping bag rated to -10°C** is essential for Everest or Manaslu treks
- A **sleeping bag liner** adds warmth and hygiene

## Packs

- **Daypack (20–25 L)**: carry during the trek (water, camera, snacks, rain gear)
- **Duffel bag (60–80 L)**: your porter carries this with the rest of your gear

## Health & Safety

- **Trekking poles**: reduce knee stress on descents by 25–30%
- **Water purification tablets or SteriPen**: drink only treated water above 3,000 m
- **Altitude medication**: Diamox (acetazolamide) — consult your doctor before travel
- **Blister kit**: Compeed blister plasters, sports tape, needle
- **Sunscreen SPF 50+**: UV intensity doubles at altitude

## Electronics

- **Portable power bank**: lodges above 3,500 m often charge for charging
- **Head torch with spare batteries**: power cuts are common; necessary for early summit mornings
- **Camera with extra memory cards**: you will take more photos than you expect

## What to Leave Behind

- Jeans and cotton clothing
- High heels or fashion trainers
- More than 3 books (heavy; leave one for the teahouse book exchange)
- Full-size toiletries — decant into 100 ml travel containers
author: Marcus Webb
